Quick note before we start — I’m back after a long break. Life happened, work happened, and the drafts folder got embarrassing. New plan: two articles a week. Hold me to it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Fili Santillán on UnsplashI write Vue at my day job. I’ve shipped React. I’ve suffered Angular. So when I say Svelte and SolidJS feel like where frontend is actually heading, it’s not fanboy energy — it’s fatigue talking. Fatigue with re-renders, with dependency arrays, with explaining to a junior why the component ran four times when nothing changed.
Non-members link.
Let me walk you through how these two frameworks differ and why developers keep quietly migrating toward them.
The problem they both solve: the virtual DOM was a workaround, not a destination
React’s big idea in 2013 was the virtual DOM. Your UI is a function of state; when state changes, React re-runs your component, builds a new virtual tree, diffs it against the old one, and patches the real DOM with the differences.
This was genuinely clever — in 2013. It made UI predictable. But notice what’s actually happening: your component function runs again even when it doesn’t need to, a whole in-memory tree gets rebuilt, and then a diffing…
